briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 31, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Escalating Domestic and International Climate Threats and Warnings
sections.content: Today, severe threats due to climate change have been reported both domestically and internationally. Domestically, climate change has led to an earlier food poisoning season and a salmonella alert, and future water shortages are predicted to surge 8 times current levels [1, 2]. Internationally, research indicates that Antarctica has become significantly more sensitive to climate change since approximately 1 million years ago, and Türkiye faces a risk of freshwater scarcity due to climate change and population growth [28, 31, 37]. Furthermore, warnings have emerged that rising temperatures could pose a greater public health threat to human health, including heart disease, and an existential threat to plant life [34, 35, 36]. These reports once again remind us of the widespread impact of climate threats on our daily lives and ecosystems.

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Policy and Research Trends
sections.content: In the international community, various research findings have been published alongside policy discussions on climate change response. Notably, concerns about ensuring information transparency were raised as the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) proposed repealing climate-related disclosure regulations, leading to the publication of critical editorials [25, 27]. Meanwhile, the importance of preparing for storms in a new era has been emphasized through traces of climate change, and new information about climate change is being revealed through Amazon tree-ring research [29, 32]. In the Asia-Pacific region, movements to understand the link between climate change and statelessness and to explore response measures have also been observed [33].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Expansion of Education
sections.content: Various local governments and institutions across the country are actively pursuing carbon neutrality practices. Institutions such as Seobu Lifelong Education Center, Yongin City, and Gyeongnam Office of Education are operating carbon neutrality campaigns and environmental education programs during Environmental Education Week, encouraging citizen participation [4, 7, 14]. Gwangmyeong City's '1.5-Degree Climate Volunteers' are leading everyday carbon neutrality through field learning, including visits to solar power plants [5, 10], and Cheongju City is promoting carbon neutrality practices through citizen challenges like 'Green Today' and 'Green Light Race' [8, 9]. Yongin City has expanded benefits for carbon neutrality practices, such as providing local currency for light rail use, and Hyundai Department Store held an eco-friendly event offering incentives for using reusable shopping bags [13, 15].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerated Domestic Renewable Energy Transition and Industry Trends
sections.content: Positive news regarding the renewable energy transition continues both domestically and internationally. Europe's largest data center hub is pursuing a strategy that leverages renewable energy accessibility as a competitive advantage [16, 24], and Korea Southern Power announced its goal to supply a total of 11.2 GW of renewable energy by 2040 [17]. Korea Western Power was recognized for its capabilities, receiving the 'Industrial Promotion Grand Prize' from the Korean Society for New and Renewable Energy, and Korea Midland Power is actively pursuing solar power projects in Sejong City, demonstrating the vigorous energy transition efforts of public power generation companies [18, 19]. Furthermore, the Lee Jae-myung administration is accelerating a 'Renewable Energy Renaissance,' with public power generation companies pursuing RE100 targets through 'Industrial Complex Solar Power,' and a 'Climate Agreement' was also signed in the Dangjin region to utilize renewable energy instead of coal [21, 23].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of climate threats, along with various domestic and international efforts to overcome them. This is a critical time when everyone's attention and action become even more crucial for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
